description:
A mobile build engineer has noticed that some utility shell scripts inside the company’s repository are committed without the executable bit.  
    Your job is to correct the permissions and leave an auditable trace – **but you must accomplish everything with a single compound shell command** (i.e. paste-once, hit ↵-once).

    Environment already present in the container:

    1. Directory layout  
       • /home/user/mobile-pipelines/  
         ├── scripts/  
         │   ├── deploy_android.sh  (currently *not* executable)  
         │   ├── build_ios.sh       (currently *not* executable)  
         │   ├── cleanup.sh         (currently *not* executable)  
         │   └── readme.md          (should stay untouched)  
         └── logs/  
             └── perm_fix.log       (this file exists and is empty)

    2. Permissions (before you begin)  
       • The three “.sh” files are 0644 (-rw-r--r--)  
       • readme.md is 0644  
       • /home/user/mobile-pipelines/logs/perm_fix.log is 0644 and writable to you.

    Task requirements

    a. Create a **single one-liner** that:
       1. Recursively searches only inside /home/user/mobile-pipelines/scripts for files ending in “.sh”.  
       2. For every such file that does **not** already have the executable bit set for the owner, add mode 755 (rwxr-xr-x).  
       3. For every file whose permission was actually changed in step 2, append **exactly one** line to /home/user/mobile-pipelines/logs/perm_fix.log using the precise format below.  
       4. Leave everything else (non-“.sh” files, already-executable scripts, directory permissions) unchanged.

    b. Log line format (must match this pattern **exactly**, automated tests perform a strict text comparison apart from the timestamp field):

        YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS | ADDED_EXEC | <relative-path-from-scripts-directory>

       • Timestamp must be in 24-hour format, zero-padded, taken from the moment you run the command.  
       • “relative-path-from-scripts-directory” means omit the leading “/home/user/mobile-pipelines/scripts/”.  
         Example (for illustration only):  
         2025-04-03 09:14:27 | ADDED_EXEC | deploy_android.sh

    What the verifier will check

    1. Exactly three new lines exist at the bottom of /home/user/mobile-pipelines/logs/perm_fix.log after your command completes.  
    2. Each line follows the exact template above and lists, in **alphabetical order**, the three script file names:  
         build_ios.sh  
         cleanup.sh  
         deploy_android.sh  
       (The timestamp part can vary; any value is accepted as long as it is a valid numeric timestamp in the given format.)  
    3. Permissions of the three “.sh” files have changed from 0644 to 0755.  
    4. No other files’ permissions have been altered.  
    5. Only a single shell command was entered by the agent.

    Hints (do NOT paste these literally; they are only conceptual clues):  
    • You might find “find”, “chmod”, “printf/echo”, and “xargs” or the “-exec” action useful.  
    • Remember that “chmod –change” can emit useful output indicating whether a change occurred.  
    • Alphabetical ordering can be achieved either before the chmod operation or while generating log lines.

    Once you have executed your one-liner and verified it worked, you are finished.
truth:
Initial state (before agent starts):

    1. File paths & permissions  
       /home/user/mobile-pipelines/scripts/deploy_android.sh   0644  
       /home/user/mobile-pipelines/scripts/build_ios.sh        0644  
       /home/user/mobile-pipelines/scripts/cleanup.sh          0644  
       /home/user/mobile-pipelines/scripts/readme.md           0644  
       /home/user/mobile-pipelines/logs/perm_fix.log           0644, empty file

    2. Expected final state:

       a. Permissions  
          deploy_android.sh   0755  
          build_ios.sh        0755  
          cleanup.sh          0755  
          readme.md           0644  (unchanged)

       b. /home/user/mobile-pipelines/logs/perm_fix.log  
          • Must contain *exactly* three new lines appended, in this order:  
              (TIMESTAMP_1) | ADDED_EXEC | build_ios.sh  
              (TIMESTAMP_2) | ADDED_EXEC | cleanup.sh  
              (TIMESTAMP_3) | ADDED_EXEC | deploy_android.sh  
          • The agent’s actual timestamps are allowed to differ; test harness will regex-match  
            “^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2} \d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2} \| ADDED_EXEC \| (build_ios\.sh|cleanup\.sh|deploy_android\.sh)$”  
            and confirm alphabetical ordering of the filenames.  
          • No additional lines may be present before or after these three.

       c. Exactly one shell command must be captured in the terminal session history (the harness counts prompt-returns).

    3. The alphabetical ordering requirement means the agent has to output lines for  
         build_ios.sh, then cleanup.sh, then deploy_android.sh – matching ASCII order.

    4. The agent runs without root; all directories are owned by user:user and writable.
